# GOLDp

> Polaris's gold-tracking pAsset. What GOLDp is, how it differs from centralized gold tokens, and how users can hold, issue, or earn with it.

Canonical URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/pgold
Markdown URL: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/pgold.md
Section: Understand Polaris

Documentation index: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/llms.txt
Full documentation bundle: https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/llms-full.txt

---

GOLDp is Polaris's gold pAsset.

It is designed to track the price of one ounce of gold while being backed by pETH collateral instead of physical bullion held by a centralized issuer.

GOLDp gives users gold-price exposure through Polaris contracts, without protocol-level custody, KYC-gated redemption, or issuer-controlled bullion access.

Note: GOLDp is Testnet-active on Sepolia. See [Launch Status](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/launch-status) for the current list of live pAssets.

## Why Use GOLDp

GOLDp is designed for users who want gold-denominated onchain exposure without relying on a centralized gold-token issuer or taking issuer counterparty risk.

Users can:

- Create GOLDp against pETH collateral.
- Hold GOLDp as a decentralized gold asset.
- Deposit GOLDp into an Earn Vault to earn scalable, protocol-native yield.
- Use GOLDp across supported Polaris and DeFi integrations.

## How GOLDp Is Created

GOLDp is created inside Polaris against pETH collateral.

Users can create GOLDp from the Issue tab by opening a position. GOLDp can also be created through above-peg arbitrage when market conditions make issuance attractive.

Both paths expand GOLDp supply against pETH collateral.

See [Issuing pAssets](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting), [Peg Defense](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations), and [Manage A Position](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/manage-position).

## GOLDp Compared With Centralized Gold Tokens

Centralized gold tokens rely on an issuer, vaulted bullion, redemption terms, custody arrangements, and often KYC-gated redemption.

GOLDp removes the physical custody and issuer redemption layer at the protocol level.

Instead, GOLDp is backed by pETH collateral and enforced by Polaris contracts. Users are choosing onchain collateral and oracle-based pricing over issuer custody and bullion redemption.

In simple terms:

- Centralized gold tokens depend on an issuer and vault.
- GOLDp depends on Polaris contracts, pETH collateral, and gold reference pricing.
- Centralized gold redemption can involve KYC, fees, minimums, and operational constraints.
- GOLDp access is permissionless at the protocol level.

## Strategy Note

Issuing GOLDp is not automatically the same as shorting gold.

Your final exposure depends on what you do after creating or acquiring GOLDp.

If you hold GOLDp: You have gold-price exposure.

If you issue GOLDp and hold the GOLDp: Your GOLDp debt and GOLDp balance can offset each other, while your position remains exposed to pETH collateral.

If you issue GOLDp and sell it for another asset: You are taking on short-gold exposure against that asset.

If you LP GOLDp against another gold asset: You can retain gold exposure while earning additional yield from LP fees on your gold.

## Peg Support

GOLDp peg support comes from issuance above its gold reference value and redemptions below it.

Those flows expand or contract supply through arbitrage, and Polaris also uses them as the onchain signal for autonomous interest rates.

See [Peg Defense](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations) and [Interest Rates](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/interest-rates).

## Earn With GOLDp

GOLDp holders can deposit into the GOLDp Earn Vault to earn protocol-native yield from issuer interest and liquidation gains.

The Earn Vault is also the first-loss backstop for GOLDp liquidations, so deposits carry liquidation-processing risk.

See [Earn Vaults](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield).

## Main Risks

GOLDp reduces issuer and physical custody risk compared with centralized gold tokens, but it still carries protocol risk.

Main risks include smart-contract risk, pETH collateral price risk, liquidation risk for issuers, issuance and redemption exposure for open positions, Earn Vault risk for depositors, oracle risk, MEV, frontend risk, and Ethereum execution risk.

GOLDp also depends on gold reference pricing. If the relevant price feeds fail, become stale, or are manipulated, GOLDp pricing and liquidations can be affected.

Read [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure).

## Next Steps

- [Issue](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/using-app/issue): Create GOLDp against pETH collateral.
- [Earn Vaults](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/yield): Deposit GOLDp to earn from protocol activity.
- [Peg Defense](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/redemptions-liquidations): Understand issuance and redemptions.
- [Interest Rates](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/minting/interest-rates): Understand how GOLDp rates respond to peg pressure.
- [Risk Disclosure](https://tokenbrice.github.io/polaris-docs/resources/risk-disclosure): Understand oracle, collateral, and protocol risks.
